cataloging_subject.label: Lucidarie
cataloging_subject.scope_note: One anonymous late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century Middle English prose adaptation of part of the Latin Elucidarium, preserved in two early-fifteenth-century Cambridge manuscripts as Lucidarie and Lucistrye. The work omits Latin Book III, selectively adapts much of Book I and a small set of Book II questions, and adds fourteen question-and-answer units. CME00140 represents Friedrich Schmitt's complete thirty-five-page edited text and apparatus plus his title page, but omits the edition's xxix-page German introduction. Medieval work, Latin source, manuscript witnesses, modern edition, digital encoding, and repository artifact remain distinct descriptive layers.

catalog_summary.title: Lucidarie
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; first attested in two early-fifteenth-century witnesses; first verified publication in 1909
catalog_summary.region: England; no place of composition is established, and the Northamptonshire localization of the first St John's scribe is retained only as witness-level dialect evidence

form_statements.description: Continuous prose dialogue between disciple and master.

scope.description: The complete Middle English work.
Master-disciple question-and-answer form addressing Christian doctrine.
Instructional dialogue intended to clarify doctrine and support lay religious understanding.
Selective translation, paraphrase, rearrangement, and original or externally derived additions relative to the Latin Elucidarium.
Vernacular prose on theology, devotion, ecclesiology, and moral instruction.
Overarching subject of the master-disciple instruction.
God, Trinity, creation, and the created order in the opening adaptation.
Angelology, demonology, creation and fall of humanity, and consequences of sin.
Incarnation, life and passion of Christ, redemption, and ascension.
Sacramental presence, reception, priestly status, and corrupt clergy.
Criticism of clerical wealth and vice, secular correction of corrupt priests, religious orders, and ideals of Christian poverty.

assertion.notes: The presence of reformist criticism does not by itself license a categorical Lollard genre or subject label.

scope.description: Church corruption, precursors of Antichrist, tribulation, and the end of the world.
The source-derived Book II questions and the two added final exchanges on grace.
Intercession and the final response's image of Mary, Christ, martyrs, and saints as tokens of divine grace.

summaries.value: The Lucidarie is an anonymous Middle English prose dialogue in which a disciple questions a master about Christian doctrine. It selectively adapts much of Book I and a small portion of Book II of the Latin Elucidarium, omits Book III, and inserts fourteen additional exchanges. Subjects range from the Trinity, creation, angels, the Fall, incarnation, redemption, Eucharist, and priesthood to clerical corruption, religious poverty, Antichrist, sin, free will, grace, Mary, and the saints. Although earlier scholarship identified the work as Lollard, Anna Lewis argues that its mixture of orthodox and potentially heterodox positions makes that label misleading; possible influence is not converted into a categorical work classification.

scope.description: The abstract Middle English work.

summaries.value: The opening follows the Latin Book I dialogue selectively through God and the Trinity, creation, angels and demons, humanity and the Fall, redemption, Christ's life and passion, ascension, Eucharist, priesthood, and corrupt clergy. Near the end, twelve added question-and-answer units are interleaved with a Book II question on social or ecclesiastical orders. The final segment adapts Book II questions on evil, sin, and free will, then ends with two further added exchanges on whether every willing petitioner may receive grace. The manuscripts themselves do not divide the work into books or chapters; the two representation segments below summarize source alignment established by Schmitt, Lewis, and Marx.

scope.description: High-level intellectual contents without treating Schmitt's imposed chapter headings as medieval structure.

summaries.value: CME00140's XML language attribute lists Middle English and German because the encoding preserves Schmitt's German title page, apparatus, and supplied notes. Work-level language is Middle English only. The XML's two books, thirty-four chapters, 494 notes, and J/U milestones describe the 1909 editorial representation; neither witness carries the book and chapter divisions. Schmitt's title Die mittelenglische Version des Elucidariums des Honorius Augustodunensis is therefore an edition title, not the preferred title of the anonymous medieval work.

scope.description: Cataloging boundary between work and representation layers.

extent.description: One prose work. No intrinsic medieval section count is established because neither witness has book or chapter divisions. For representation analysis, CME00140 contains two DIV2 book wrappers with thirty-four DIV3 chapters (31 + 3), thirty-five HEAD elements, 291 P elements, thirty-six PB elements, 494 NOTE elements, seventy-four manuscript MILESTONE elements (thirty J folio markers and forty-four U page markers), and 847 HI elements. Those are Schmitt/XML editorial metrics, not an item or medieval-structure count.

content_parts.label: Lucidarie
content_parts.summary: The complete surviving Middle English master-disciple dialogue as represented by the more complete J text and the defective U copy.
notes: J and U likely descend from a common exemplar; neither is identified as the other's source.
J is more careful and more complete, but James's description of its ending as unfinished remains unresolved.

content_parts.label: Selective adaptation of Latin Book I with added dialogue
content_parts.summary: A highly selective and increasingly revised adaptation of Latin Book I, covering major doctrinal topics and incorporating twelve added question-and-answer units near the end; one Latin Book II question is inserted among the additions.

locators.value: pp. 1-32, ending immediately before Schmitt's Liber 2 sequence on p. 33
Selective Book I questions plus Book II question 8 and twelve added exchanges; detailed mapping in Marx Table 2 and Lewis pp. 213-214

notes: This is an analytical source-alignment segment, not a manuscript book division.
Marx describes eleven additions followed by one further added exchange around the inserted Book II material; Lewis describes twelve new questions in the altered Book I sequence.

content_parts.label: Selected Latin Book II questions and added grace ending
content_parts.summary: The closing sequence adapts Latin Book II material on evil, sin, and free will, then adds two final exchanges affirming that grace is available to those who ask.

locators.value: Book II questions 3, 4, 6, and 7 in Lewis's analysis, followed by two added questions on grace

notes: This is an analytical source-alignment segment, not a manuscript book division.

content_parts.label: Schmitt's 1909 title, structure, and apparatus represented in CME00140
content_parts.summary: Modern German title-page matter, imposed two-book and thirty-four-chapter source alignment, German notes, J/U variants and milestones, expanded abbreviations, supplied punctuation, and other editorial typography surrounding the medieval text.

locators.value: FRONT/DIV1 title page; BODY HEAD, NOTE, MILESTONE, PB, and HI layers
title page and printed pp. 1-35; dissertation introduction pp. i-xxix omitted

notes: This part documents the representation layer and is excluded from the one-work intrinsic extent count.
Schmitt's title and the U-M inherited Honorius attribution are not promoted into preferred work title or target-work authorship.

related_works.title: Elucidarium
notes: The three-book Latin dialogue is traditionally attributed to Honorius Augustodunensis. The Middle English work is partial, selective, rearranged, and supplemented with fourteen added exchanges.
No physical Latin exemplar or exact recension is identified.

open_questions.question: Why does Lewis state that the Lucidarie includes six questions from Latin Book II when her detailed sequence and Marx's Table 2 identify II.8 plus II.3-4, 6-7, a set of five numbered Latin questions?
next_steps: Collate Schmitt pp. 29-35 against Lefevre's numbered Latin questions.
Determine whether a split, compound, or alternate numbering convention explains the discrepancy.
Retain qualitative wording in catalog summaries until the count is reconciled.

open_questions.question: Who produced the Lucidarie, where was it composed, and which Latin Elucidarium recension or physical exemplar did the adapter use?
next_steps: Collate distinctive readings and question order against current Latin Elucidarium editions and stemmatic scholarship.
Search for shared lexical and doctrinal features in other late Middle English religious dialogues without converting witness dialect into composition place.
Retain anonymous translator-adapter responsibility and England-only cultural origin until direct evidence emerges.

open_questions.question: Is the J text physically or textually incomplete at f. 16r, or did James use 'ends unfinished' only for the absence of a formal explicit?
next_steps: Inspect f. 16r-16v, the end of the second quire, and any catchword or erasure evidence.
Compare J's terminal response with U, the Latin source sequence, and other Middle English Elucidarium versions.
Do not call J absolutely complete merely because it is more complete than U.

open_questions.question: What are the exact textual and physical extents of U's lacunae, and how do Schmitt's later page-number references map to the quire losses described by James and Lewis?
next_steps: Obtain images of ff. 79v-101v and the adjacent quire boundaries from Cambridge University Library.
Crosswalk U pp. 158-202, present foliation, Schmitt's apparatus gaps after pp. 176 and 192, and Lewis's quire reconstruction.
Record physical lacunae separately from material omitted by the Middle English adapter.

open_questions.question: How should the Lucidarie's mixture of reformist, orthodox, and potentially heterodox positions be situated more precisely without restoring the misleading categorical label 'Lollard text'?
next_steps: Compare the added exchanges with securely dated reformist and Wycliffite theological texts at the level of individual doctrines and phrasing.
Study the distinct compilation contexts of J and U as evidence of reception rather than proof of composition origin.
Keep Lollardy out of positive genre, subject, and tag fields unless a future controlled term explicitly represents scholarly debate rather than categorical identity.

notes: Lucidarie is preferred because it is the J title and common scholarly title; Lucistrye is retained as U's equally real witness title.
The long German title in CME00140 is Schmitt's edition title, not an original or preferred title of the medieval work.

Honorius Augustodunensis is the traditional or attributed author of the Latin Elucidarium only. U-M and Hathi catalog headings that place Honorius in the author position are not transferred to target-work responsibility.
The work is one prose dialogue. Schmitt's two books and thirty-four chapters are modern source alignment, and the content parts record them only as analytical representation segments.
German and Latin material in CME00140 is editorial, source, or quotation-layer content; the work-level language statement remains Middle English.

The Lollard label is deliberately excluded from positive classification and tags because Lewis finds it misleading. This does not erase reformist content or foreclose more precise future doctrinal analysis.
J and U likely share a common exemplar. J is Schmitt's base and more complete; U is a collateral defective witness, not a derivative of J.
CME00140 encodes the complete thirty-five-page Middle English text selection of Schmitt's edition but not the complete printed dissertation, whose German introduction occupies pp. i-xxix.
